[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
comments.content:
Good Evening,

May the upcoming astrological reset of Aries bring welcome new opportunities, and ends to what you would have less of!

To Share: Gene Wolfe has been an occasional topic of conversation around here. If you haven't checked out The Wizard Knight, I can strongly recommend it. The ending felt a bit rushed, and I get the impression that Wolfe needs "room to breathe" to be at his full powers. Maybe would have been better as a trilogy of like-sized books, rather than a duology, but still well worth the read. A masterful blend of a hermetic worldview, Arthurian legend, and Germanic myth.

To Ask: A friend was taking a look at the traditional folktale "The Smith and the Devil." In most attested versions, it's pretty Christian: the supernatural being with which the smith makes a deal is literally the devil, often the intercession of Saint Peter is needed to bring things to a happy ending, and so forth. That said, it's apparently a very old story, dating back maybe to Proto-Indo-European times, suggesting a non/pre-Christian version of the story. My friend is trying to unravel what that pre-Christian version might have looked like, and I'd appreciate any pointers from our host or the commentariat on likely myths/mythical figures to check out for comparison (Hephaistos and Weland/Volund are clear smith figures, but I'm not familiar with Indic or Celtic versions, for example).

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content: Hmm! I'm not familiar with older versions of the folktale. I assume your friend has already read Mircea Eliade's book The Forge and the Crucible, with its discussions of the magical dimensions of smithcraft; that's the only resource that comes to mind just now.

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content:
Not sure if he has, but I'll be sure to mention it, thank you!

(Also one of the few Eliade books that wasn't already on my list for myself, so I'll amend that error as well.)

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content: You may be amused to know that it's the first book by Eliade I ever read.

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content:
I Think Rudyard Kipling had tale about Weland..(not certain) but it isn't Old.
And Sir Terry Prachitt, even Less old

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content: Thanks nonetheless!

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content: To Make Sure: have you looked into Folktales in the Indo-European Tradition?

replies.title: Re: The Wizard Knight and Question on "The Smith and the Devil"
replies.content: Thanks for checking, but yes, that was the starting place for both me and my friend - it's a great book!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1 - There seems to be a New Thought adept at work in the memeworks busily banging out Amelia videos for YouTube. (Nevermind the volume and quality suggesting deep pockets somewhere.) Since working-class UK feels downtrodden, something like Skip Downing’s encouragement to be a creator rather than a victim looks to be in play. The memes I’ve been noticing on YouTube seem aimed at reawakening the dormant sense of people and place so that the citizens’ can be reminded that they do have the means to change their government to suit their needs.

Specifically, examining any collection of Amelia memes suggests Paul Ekman’s discrete emotional theory is being used to lead voters along the path of attention, anger, fear, outrage, questions, answers and solutions to the positions of Restore Britain as laid out in their mission and philosophy video on their YouTube channel.

So – do you and the commentariat a rather brainy New Thought campaign to win voters to an upstart party? Or – am I over-connecting disparate dots?

Also, have you or your commentariat developed any idea who might be the divinity inspiring Amelia and her memes?


2 - My interest in Amelia bumps into my curiosity about my American nature and some discussion here about how inextricably British the practice of Druidry is. A friend of mine from the UK recently became US citizen, so he’s able to point out how English both Ohio is and I am. (Well, we do speak with a broken-in West Country accent, so…) Anyway - I almost knocked him over by standing up to shake hands. Despite being not much bigger than he is, I take up much more personal space, and his reaction to colliding with mine looked like he’d walked into a glass door… Which leaves me to wonder if my misadventures in Druidry have a solidifying effect on my personal space. Umm… does it? And – is this effect related to the way elderly men violate my personal space or just want my attention at a noticeably higher rate than my fellow hardware store clerks? I’m thinking so, but coincidence is not causality…


3 - And – Since HPL comes up here now and again… While looking for Amelia memes, an AI music video, “Down at the Crossroads - Memphis Blues - Uncle Sam's Blues Featuring ‪clickthecat‬,” (you’ll want the five minute version with the spoken intro) happened by… leaving me to wonder about Nyarlathotep’s interest in the blues how widely influential Lovecraft is, and why his source material is enjoying a resurgence.

2) How very odd. I'll be interested to hear suggestions from those who, not being as autistic as I am, might have a clue.

3) To the best of my knowledge, the idea that Nyarlathotep is a blues fan was my own invention -- once I'd decided to redefine the Great Old Ones as the old gods of nature and started retconning them into folklore and myth, equating the Crawling Chaos with the crossroads devil of blues legend was an easy choice, and his musical tastes followed from there. Lovecraft, though, has been hugely influential in American popular culture since the 1970s. His source material? Most of what he wrote came to him in dreams...

replies.title: (no subject)

3 - I recall but can't locate some jazz-themed (okay, not blues) post-HPL stories and RPG supplements. I never paid post-HPL much attention because everything seemed somehow "off" until your corrected texts appeared on tablets of an unknown metal found in a quarry in Maple County, Ohio. Over to the commentariat for the strange background music of post-HPL horror.

3a - May I argue that while Lovecraft did his best thinking while asleep, he frequently fell asleep reading while drinking a cocktail of Puritan bitter dregs and the bottom-run absinthe of the Decadents? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Of course. They've bought into the official propaganda regarding magic, and so don't have the least idea how practical it is, or how deeply it interfaces with psychology. Can't have the proles figuring out that they can free their minds and change their lives so easily! As for why they find their way to magic anyway, why, magic is so deeply hardwired into our nervous systems that it's a wonder more people don't get there on their own. Did you know that in medieval Latin, the word sapiens was the standard word for wizard? (The words mean the same thing: "wise person.") Thus any literate medieval person would have interpreted Homo sapiens as "a person who is a wizard."

2) As I recall, the standard setting for the Call of Cthulhu TTRPG was the 1920s. As for those tablets, the metal's only unknown to those who don't know their eldritch lore. It's astonishing how few people recognize ordinary Atlantean orichalcum when they see it... ;-

3) Don't forget Greek mythology. He was a crazed fan of Greek myth as a child, to the extent of building an altar in the back garden and pouring libations (of water, granted) to Zeus and Athena. As for the Puritans, I see their beliefs as a transitional phenomenon -- part of the process by which they discarded Christianity and embraced the anthropolatrous religion of Progress. Such transitional stages don't keep well.

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I am trying to understand something in my life from an occult perspective and I am not sure which model within occultism to use.

The issue is I always run into trouble with father figures. More specifically, they never seem to listen to anything I have to say and almost always underestimate my abilities. My own biological father did this to a comical degree. For example, he seemed utterly baffled that I wanted to pursue graduate education when I spent my whole childhood obsessed with this idea. I have since then experienced the same thing with numerous older men in positions of authority over me. I myself am a very academically inclined individual and these people often see me as ignorant while peers and for that matter everyone see the opposite in me.

If I use karma to understand this I might say I abused authority over another in a past life and now need to pay this karmic debt by realizing the error.

Yeats vision might say that I am at a place on the lunar wheel that calls for me to lay aside my own personality and start moving towards more collective concerns.

Jung might say I am projecting my shadow and really there is something bad yet unrecognized in these men that I subconsciously see in myself.

So what do you do when you are trying to discern between competing occult paradigms? Do you use mediation? Prayer? Divination? Or what?

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content: Paradigms are overrated, and consciously held and constructed paradigms are even more so. It's far more important simply to accept that your experience is what it is, acknowledge that you don't yet know what it means, and get on with life.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content: Point taken. Its unproductive to spend too much time in the purely abstract. Still, different paradigms give you different perspectives on what to expect next, so I thought there might be some value in distinguishing which one seems to be operative here. But yes, maybe the most productive thing to do is recognize I seem to struggle with one particular personality type and instead start working out how ways to deal with the specific set of circumstances I encounter in the future.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content: Exactly. My experience is that settling on a consciously held paradigm can make it harder to get to the kind of realization that actually helps -- the point where you stop cold and say, "Oh, okay, now I get it! That's what it was about all along!" That comes from experience and reflection; it can be furthered by thinking about the various paradigms, but it's hindered by premature commitment to any one option.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content:
Ok, so yes, we do all think in narratives, and those narratives limit what we are able to think. The narratives you have opted for (continue to indulge in, to put it more harshly) can leave you trapped in fixed perspectives on what to expect next. Those narratives will continue recreating similar circumstances, since that is both what they do and what your expectations have now been limited to. The question then is how to get out of a box that you are unwittingly keeping as securely closed as possible.

I'm intrigued that you recognize the power of narrative framing to restrict your perspective, yet continue searching for an alternate narrative framing with a more liberating fit. It is always a truly magical moment when you stumble upon some overlooked mythic truth that completely recontextualizes how you've been relating to your past/family/self/etc., but those transformative myths are inevitably the ones we've been most assiduously overlooking, so they're not likely to be in our go-to list. Which paradigms are not even showing up on your radar currently? The ones you find the least promising may well turn out to be the box cutter you've been looking for.

The particular framings you led the witness with (that one's not to be harsh, since we all do that much more than we imagine) are each a bit deterministic. Karma, Yeats' schematic, and Jung's shadow projection are all fated burdens we cannot hope to escape from, similar to one's astrological chart or one's family dynamic. Since you're looking to escape from endless repeats of cycles originating within your family, you might want to consider framings that do allow for escape and reimagining of one's habitual nature. A wonderful quote from Harold and Maude comes to mind, "Consistency is not really a human trait."

How inconsistent and out of character can you let yourself be? Or stated another way, what parts of how you imagine yourself to be would you have to let go of in order to try out unimaginable narratives, perspectives, and paradigms? More importantly, are you sure you want to? We always end up relinquishing much more than just our burdens when we take up occult practice in earnest. Do you really want to discover that your father, your family, your peers, and your Self are not remotely how you were imagining them to be? Do you really want the dizzying disorientation of discerning that the framings you’ve used to keep your boxes rigidly intact can just as easily be used as doorways to step through?

—Christophe

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content:
You wouldn't happen to be Gen-X - born between 1964 and 1983 - would you?
That treatment is a distressingly common habit of Baby Boomers with the worst offenders having been idealistic hippies.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content: Close. I was born in 1987 but my parents were baby boomers. They had me in their late thirties so I'm technically a millennial but I was raised like a gen x.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content: I would look at the “prince” archetype in both literature, tarot and myth. I’ve had the same problem. Also, listen to interviews of children of famous parents.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content: Sounds like a useful place to look. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content:
If it is a particularly persistent issue, it might show on your natal chart a bit.

I have similar issues and was advised it was highly likely I was a bit righteous and overbearing in a previous life by a natal astrologer and the karma was playing through in this life (based on my Jupiter). I also have a Saturn and uranus (who are both retrograde) opposite mercury (4th house) and communication in particular with authority and my original family is such an ongoing and difficult issue in my life.

replies.title: Re: How do I figure out which occult model to use?
replies.content:
It might be a class and not so much a generational issue, at least in part. I faced a similar situation growing up because I graduated from college, first in my family. A friend of my parents, a good ol'boy* from the South, criticized my decision, "why'd you do that? Think yer better than any one else?". Thing is - he was a member of the 'silent' generation and me, a boomer. So it might not be generational or even class - it might simply be age related. I would have no idea of any occult connections there might be, and now you make me wonder.

* Although he had certain opinions & expressed them freely, this friend of my parents was really a great guy, generous & kind. BTW, his daughter ended getting a college education & became a teacher. And the main reason I went to college, was to pursue options that were simply not available in a small backward town, not because I was 'smart'.

comments.title: Yeats Phase 1 & Mental Sheath Reset?
comments.content:
Since it is not a question about the second quarter, I am posting this question here.

During Phase 1 of total objectivity, does the soul's mental sheath get stripped down to its most primitive form, which then has the opportunity to develop as the soul moves through the phases?

Patrick H

Edit: Alternatively, the mental sheath just goes mostly dormant during the early primary phases.

replies.title: Re: Yeats Phase 1 & Mental Sheath Reset?
replies.content: That's a very good question which Yeats does not address at all. To the extent that he hints at an answer at all, it would be that the growth of the mental sheath is a slow process and takes multiple trips 'round the Great Wheel to take place. Thus a soul at Phase 1 the first few times around wouldn't have more than a very tentative mental sheath anyway, and a soul very late in the process of human existence would have a very different experience of Phase 1. But that's a tentative suggestion, and much development of the system would be needed to settle the matter.

comments.title: Geomancy repeated figures vs judge and witnesses
comments.content:
Recently I cast a shield chart for an omen regarding an upcoming trip that I would appreciate an extra set of eyes on:

What do I most need to know about my upcoming trip?

1st Mother: Merch
2nd Mother: Bendith Fawr
3rd Mother: Colled
4th Mother: Merch

1st Daughter: Merch
2nd Daughter: Pobl
3rd Daughter: Ffordd
4th Daughter: Mab

1st Niece: Llawenydd
2nd Niece: Tristwch
3rd Niece: Merch
4th Niece: Gwyn

RW: Carchar
LW: Carchar
Judge: Pobl

I see a pair of Carchar, a negative figure making Pobl, a neutral figure. 2 negative + 1 neutral = negative reading.

However, Carchar repeated is considered a good omen with regard to safety in travel, and in questions concerning travel it is a good omen if traveling by land. I have a flight involved, but the majority of travel is land-based. The upper chart doesn't seem very ominous - 3 of 4 nieces are favorable and the top row is 50% composed of Merch and Bendith Fawr, which are good signs, accompanied by Ffordd, a good sign for land trips, and pobl, which at least doesn't hurt.

Given world events, would it be too far-fetched to square the circle by supposing this omen is one of safety in travels by virtue of the trip being cancelled, or is there a different interpretation I'm missing?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy repeated figures vs judge and witnesses
replies.content: If I got this chart in response to that question I'd assume that the trip would be cancelled or changed significantly (Carchar) due to collective events outside my control (Pobl). The repeated figures are in my experience less important than the testimony of the judge and witnesses.

comments.title: SoP with Planetary Forces
comments.content:
Apologies if this has been asked and answered before, but is there a way of modifying the SoP to call on planetary names as one would gods?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: SoP with Planetary Forces
replies.content:
Sure. The standard symbolism is this:

East: Sun
West: Mercury
South: Mars
North: Venus
Below: Saturn
Above: Jupiter

The Moon corresponds to Spirit Within, but of course you don't invoke that seventh factor by name. since you yourself are always the central element in the SoP.

replies.title: Re: SoP with Planetary Forces
replies.content: Thank you! And would one vibrate the names?

replies.title: Re: SoP with Planetary Forces
replies.content: Up to you. If I were going to do that, I'd look up the Latin pronunciation, which is more resonant; I'd also use Mavors, the older spelling of Mars (that's MAH-wors, btw). But your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: SoP with Planetary Forces
replies.content:
Not OP.

So that would mean that we would say something like "In the great name of the planet Mercury, I invoke the Water, its gods, its spirits, and its powers. May the powers of Water bless and protect me this day and always, and further my work. May my heart be instructed by the ways of nature." in the planetary sphere of protection?

Can we also use planetary names for the opening as well or do we have to still use the names of three gods?

replies.title: Re: SoP with Planetary Forces
replies.content:
1) You could.

2) I'd recommend instead invoking the heavens and the earth in the opening, and modifying it to use just those two.

comments.title: Celtic Questions!
comments.content:
Hello JMG!

• Would it be useful to start with Learning Ritual Magic before Celtic Golden Dawn? I would not skip anything from the second course, even if perceived overlaps were found, and I don't intend to mix up any practices. My target is the Celtic Golden Dawn. However I heard LRM is very good to star off slowly, build the necessary muscle and also take the time to study the standard classics of western ritual magic. Being in no hurry, adding an informed appreciation of how CGD changes the classic GD and Kabbalah basics sound very interesting, but only if it would not be bad for the actual practice.

• I read somewhere that classic GD magic seeks more of a transcendental goal than more natural, druidic practices; That you would tend to, when following such more nature based systems, "stay here" with the natural world in your discarnate future, connecting in a different way with the beyond. Does that make any sense to you?

Thks!!

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content:
1) No, LRM uses a different symbolism entirely. The Celtic Golden Dawn is designed for the complete beginner, and you can take as much time in the first level, the Ovate Grade, as you need. (You can do the same thing with the other two grades, for that matter.)

2) Yes, but the Celtic GD system isn't a nature-based system in that sense. It's a solar path, with effects similar to any other GD system. You might compare it to the system in my book The Druid Magic Handbook to get some sense of the differences.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: Thanks! Can you talk a bit about what awaits an adept of a nature based system in his afterlife? The "staying here" part?

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: We know very little about the various states of existence that follow after material embodiment. The Chinese Taoists, who have put as much work into this as anybody (and much more than most), differentiate between celestial and terrestrial immortals. The former are those who have dispensed with the need for a material body and proceed on to realms of existence apart from this world, for which the term "heaven" would not be too mistaken a term. The latter are those who have dispensed with the need for a material body but continue to exist in this realm of existence, freed from its hindrances but still able to interact with it. Both pursue their further spiritual development in their different settings. The GD, as I interpret it, is a way toward the former state, while nature-based systems tend toward the latter.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content:
Fascinating, and at the risk of overcomplicating and/or overthinking things we can't know much about, I'd like to ask a couple follow-ups. This is also of interest to me since staying intuitively feels more appealing to me, but so far I've also found a GD/solar path to work better for me, at least in this life.

1) What would this division imply for those who've followed both paths to an advanced degree, like yourself (if that's not too personal)? Is it a matter of which path you commit to over the course of many lives?

2) Since our realm of existence will presumably have a finite lifespan, compared to those "higher" realms of the celestial path, will the terrestrial immortals have to "migrate" upwards eventually as well?

Bonus 3) This looks like a classic binary...I assume what makes the ternary is a fine theme for meditation? :)

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content:
1) One of the things I had to confront some years ago was the choice between the two. I chose the nature-centered path, after much reflection, and that's governed my work since then.

2) Sure, but the Earth will be habitable for something like 1.2 billion years, so there's plenty of time to prepare!

3) Why, yes, indeed it is!

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: Thank you, appreciate the perspective. Now that I think about it, this also brings to mind the difference between the Aesir and the Vanir in Heathen cosmology. In any case, while I'm finishing my current work no matter what, I think I'll have to do some serious reflection on this myself when it comes time to decide whether to go on to the Jarl grade in the HGD.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content:
The Heathen Golden Dawn looks like an awesome system. I wish it was around when I was studying runes some years ago. I have the book and I love how it’s laid out.

How is it going for you so far? - if you feel like sharing.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content:
Maybe I'm reading too much into a three-sentence summary of what is presumably a very large body of lore but would that imply that celestial immortals are incapable of interacting with humans still stuck in material embodiment?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: Not at all. They just interact with humans on different planes -- primarily the upper astral and mental planes, where spatial distance is less of an issue. Terrestrial immortals, by contrast, can interact with incarnate humans all the way down to the dense etheric plane.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content:
Thank you, that makes more sense.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: Would this have any implications for systems like the CGD? It's a GD system, but it's also based on Druidry, which is a nature based system; so it seems like it has a pull in both directions....

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: No, the Celtic GD is a GD system; it uses Druid Revival imagery but its energetics are solar rather than the mixed solar-telluric pattern that's standard in the more terrestrially oriented systems. Again, compare it with The Druid Magic Handbook if you want to get a sense of the difference.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: What about the Golden Section books? Are they solar or more telluric in nature?

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: They work the same balanced solar-telluric approach as The Druid Magic Handbook. All the material taught by, or inspired by, John Gilbert's work falls into that category.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Questions!
replies.content: Many thanks once more for your patience and dedication, Mr. Greer! Have a great week. :)

comments.title: Non-magical Spring Equinox Celebrations?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
At this page is the full list of all of the non-faith-specific requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts (printable version here, current to 3/13). Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests, selected from the fuller list.


May Bob W of Lake County, Ohio's treatment for cancer go well so that he may heal and recover as quickly as possible.

May Open Space be filled with the strength he needs to heal quickly from his current bout of Chicken Pox; may his will remain strong, that he does not scratch; may he be healed completely, and suffer no scarring in its wake.

May Jule from Iserlohn, Germany, who is experiencing complications in her pregnancy due to an influenza infection, recover and have a pleasant pregnancy and birth.

comments.title: Inner sound
comments.content: After doing zen meditation for about a year I became aware of a high-pitched ”inner sound” that springs up when I am silent and still. It is somewhere between a vibration and a sound and is very soothing as a meditation tool. Do you have any idea of what this might be? Or what it might signify? Thanks

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: That's a common experience with certain kinds of meditation; it's what Pythagoras called the music of the spheres. It means that you've made some progress in meditation, which is a very good thing. You might, though, see if you can find a properly trained Zen instructor -- now that you've gotten that far, you may need expert guidance to avoid some of the potential pitfalls in the intermediate and advanced stages of the work.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: Oh, so that is it! I've been experiencing this for years, while doing various spiritual practices, but also sometimes throughout the day, even right now as I sit writing this. One of my spiritual mentors said it was something like the sound of the holy spirit descending or opening. I first noticed it in his presence after doing group self inquiry, and then would notice it when I was doing buddhist meditation, Heathen Golden Dawn rituals (sometimes very intensely), and now when I practice mantra japa, or just when I'm silent and centered.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: The old Pythagoreans would say that you've stilled your mind enough to hear the music of the spheres. It's a good sign.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
Interesting! How does this differ from tinnitus? I’ve had ringing in the ears off and on for several years. A round of Chinese herbs and acupuncture took the edge off, and as a side benefit healed the digestive issues I mindlessly attributed to aging.

Still, I have (milder) intermittent ringing. It’s pretty much the soundtrack to my life these days.

Thanks,
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: Does the ringing only become apparent in quiet states, and especially in meditation? And is it soothing and comforting? Both these are standard parts of the music of the spheres.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
I wish I could answer “yes!” to both questions. It is more noticeable when things are quiet, but I don’t find it comforting. I’m just happy that it is less intrusive than it was. Several years ago, I noticed a visual sparkle or two after completing my morning Heathen LBRP/Middle Pillar, and hoped I was on track for some sort of mystical experience. Nope! Occasional sparks are common in the prolonged build up to an ocular migraine, which finally manifested in all of its glory several months later. No pain; just quite the light show for a few minutes. Fairly common among middle aged and older people. Also of note is that this can, if it happens a lot, indicate a torn retina. “Torn” I am told is painless, but it’s an emergency nonetheless. Those early sparkles warrant an initial trip to the optometrist so they can be evaluated and monitored.

I soldier on with my practices. If spectacular stuff doesn’t happen to me in this life, I’m okay with that.

OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
1) Then it's tinnitus and not the mystical experience.

2) Yes, I know about the sparkles. I get them from time to time as migraine prodromes, though if I do the right things I don't get the migraine.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
Interesting! May I ask, just out of curiosity and certainly not seeking medical advice, what you do concerning said stray sparkles?

Thanks as always,
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: Take a nap and drink plenty of fluids, especially green tea. Extra rest and hydration usually staves off migraines, for me.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
Thanks JMG! 🙏

OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
Not OP. I also have tinnitus, so good to know acupuncture helps, I will ask my acupuncturist about it.

I notice my tinnitus gets cranked up to 11 when I enter deep meditation, it can get quite loud but I stop paying attention to it. I'm pretty sure I've also noticed a quiet, steady hum during meditation, I'll have to pay attention to tomorrow when I meditate next.

Speaking of hums, I live in a semi-rural area, a hamlet. Most mornings when I wake up, I will hear a constant low hum, like an electrical device is plugged in. I would go around the house trying to figure out whether it was the fridge, etc, then open the door to see if it was a neighbour, but I could never hear it outside the house.

The mystery was compounded a month or two ago when our area lost power completely during a storm. In the windless aftermath early the next morning, with hundreds of houses around without power, I still could hear the same hum! I opened the door to check if it was someone's generator - silence. In fact, the hum disappeared when I opened the door. The main power lines into the house were presumably completely without any current.

The only candidate is that we have a fire hall nearby, about a 2 minute walk away, with a radio antenna and presumably a backup generator. That's the only thing I can think of, although I couldn't hear anything when the door was open. It's still a total mystery. My wife can't hear it at all, but her hearing isn't the best.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: It could be a natural resonant mode of the air in your house or in a confined space like the wall or basement or attic, getting pumped like a flute or a pipe organ or whistling lips by some constant just-unstable-enough-to-vibrate flow of air through a small gap driven by pressure differences (possibly from solar heating) somehow. Under this hypothesis, opening the door would change the resonant mode or remove the pressure difference driving the flow, and you should expect that opening a window would do the same thing. Depending on which chamber was resonating, the hypothesis would also suggest that you could sing at the same base frequency while inside the house with the door closed and notice a resonance, compared to singing a little higher or lower.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: I've started hearing this as well, although I'm a solo golden dawn practitioner. Any pitfalls you would advise in the case of golden dawn?

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content: Get your hearing checked to make sure it's not tinnitus. Other than that, the tradition doesn't offer much guidance.

replies.title: Re: Inner sound
replies.content:
I wonder if that's the "same thing as" the "inner sound" that the Surat Shabd yoga people are on about, along with "inner light". "Inner light" sounds like the Zen thing about vast clear luminosity.

Come to think of it, both of those feel like something whose material aspect you could partly reductionistically-articulate what was going on there with the paradigm of predictive processing -- something about the direction of flow of stimulus-derived-evidence-like influences in the web of interpretational tendencies, and certain corners the system's tuning can be driven into (in this case somehow by use of deliberate attention and training on organically arising examples of subtle baseline dynamics not drowned out by confounding variables from the usual distractions, or whatever).

comments.title: Divine Names in Mysteries of Merlin
comments.content:
Good Morning Mr. Greer and commentariat,

I'm working through the Ovate cicle of Mysteries of Merlin currently and have some questions regarding the divine names in the LRP.

I'm likely overthinking unhelpfully, but if someone can offer some insights or context, that would be appreciated anyways.

1. Is there some more information about HEU'C and SYLW?
I assumed SYLW is connected to the sun, but for HEU'C I have absolutely no idea who / what it would be connected to? I only assume it should be connected to a deity, because the other two directions get a name from the deities associated with the stations on the wheel.

2. I saw somewhere a template you posted for creating ones own LRP and there IIRC it was stated to put two gods and two godesses with the godesses at E / W. Assuming SYLW is corresponding to a goddess as the name is opposite ELEN, would you be willing to elaborate why you did it the other way for the CGD? That just got me curious.

3. My mind somehow expects Elen and Esus to be associated with the other's direction as that would make them correspond more to the elements. What am I missing here?

Thanks for your time.

replies.title: Re: Divine Names in Mysteries of Merlin
replies.content:
1) Heu'c is a variant form of Hu Gadarn, Hu the Mighty, who appears in Druid Revival literature and also Welsh mythology. Sulw is a variant of Sul, the goddess of the hot springs at Bath, called Sulis Minerva by the Romans.

2) Because it worked better that way. Keep in mind, first, that the templates I post are suggestions rather than laws, and second, that I deliberately do things differently in different books and other resources, in an attempt to head off fundamentalism of the "JMG said it, I believe it, that settles it" variety before it can get started.

3) What a fine theme for mediation! I mean that quite literally. If something in the teachings doesn't make sense to you, study it, meditate on it, treat it as a riddle that has something important to teach.

replies.title: Re: Divine Names in Mysteries of Merlin
replies.content:
Thanks for your response!
I will add #3 to my list of meditation prompts then, that sounds like a helpful idea.

comments.title: The Higher Genius and Occult Medicine
comments.content:
I have been working some practices that help you connect with my Higher Genius that I do once every other week. It has been a moving practic so when you mentioned the orphic hymn to the personal daemon as a daily devotional practice I jumped on the idea and have been doing it daily. The language took me some effort, and the effort made me think.

I notice, perhaps out of my catholic upbringing, that the way in which deity is addressed here in the hymn is not very different from the catholic way of doing it. For example, the use of the words dread and terrible, revenge and tortures dire, refer much more to the sense of fear inspiring awe of something much bigger and powerful than you. Someone who can decide your fate and damn you if you push too hard against her will. I found that very instructive, because even though I approach deities with respect I do seem to more generally approach them as I would approach Jesus: forgiving and compassionate. I don't think that attitude is that useful when it comes to deities that are not of compassion because it tends to foster a response of fear in the devotee when a compassionate reply is not received. Ehm, like a child scolded by a parent instead of, say, a coworker to which you respond to. That could very easily be mishandled. For example, finding such deity dangerous or worse, evil, if one is self centered enough and unable to think differently about deities. Somehow that rings a bell!

I can see, on the other hand, how when praying to God it can lead to groveling and asking for forgiveness instead of manning up and doing what needs to be done in a world where the lower self is not the center, and the deity is not there to take care of you. I've noticed you mentioning this, but I don't think I had been able to discern what it meant. Useful, so very useful. The religion I was born into needs to bring more emphasis in its catechism in what I guess is referred as the fear of God, it would save everyone from so much trouble. It makes me think of some of the deities I've had trouble connecting with, like Saturn. I can almost picture him working, wondering what that screeching noise coming out of his devices has been all this long, only to say: Oh, pity, another stubborn soul got stuck on the gears of my clock, lets free him now. I chuckle now at that thought.

My first question today is. Would you be able to say a few words on this notion of deity as terrible and dreadful beings from the Neoplatonic point of view and their relationship with us?

Secondly, towards the end it's mentioned attending to the sacred rites. What is this referring to?

And a third question if I may. I am on my way back from Asia and I did a stop in Malaysia. One of the things I did was going to the Museum of Islamic Arts. Besides my jaw hitting the floor every other showcase from the staggering beauty and precision of Malay and Islamic handicrafts, I became aware of something I was not from the Ancient World. Through trade routes, Malay and Islamic healing modalities learned to use the Galenic humoral system and seem to have developed it considerably into their own traditiod. And there are loads of manuscripts about them. I bought a 3kg book just of summaries of them. I am very interested in occult and energetic physiology and anatomy partly because it is one of the fields that I consider need more development and reconstruction in our tradition. If you had to trace a workpath for a medium term research and experimental project, say, 4-6 months of work into this topic: What tools would be useful for correlation with what we do have in the west? What questions would you ask yourself? One of the things I would like to know is if they were able to workout an anatomy and system of working with the dense etheric body and if they applied alchemy or herbology into it.

replies.title: Re: The Higher Genius and Occult Medicine
replies.content:
1) To my mind it's crucial to remember that the gods are vaster and mightier than we can imagine. The dim reflections of them that show up in our mythologies and traditions are inadequate, to use no stronger word, as expressions of their true power, might, and scale. Nor can they be anything else, since (to return to a favorite metaphor) our chance of understanding the gods is roughly on a par with the chance that the dust mites in Mozart's wig could understand his music. Fear is an appropriate response to this. The Bible is spot on when it says "the fear of God is the beginning of wisdom" -- not the end of it, or even the intermediate stage of it, but the beginning. If you don't shiver at least a little when you contemplate the gods you're missing an important reality.

The guardian genius or daimon -- that's spelled "guardian angel" in Christian-influenced traditions -- is not on the same scale, but it's still bigger, wiser, and mightier than the individual it guards. The Orphic hymn focuses on the idea that the guardian genius is also the one that dispenses what Eastern traditions call karma -- "revenge and tortures dire" refer to that. You're basically saying to the genius, "Help me work with you so I don't have to get socked in the face too often."

2) That's the point of the sacred rites. Remember that the Orphic hymns were originally written to be sung at celebrations of mystery initiations! The same principle applies, however, whenever you pray and make offerings to the gods. You're asking your guardian genius to be present and take part in the courteous and hospitable interaction that prayer and offerings are meant to bring about.

3) I'm probably the last person to ask, because I don't plan out workpaths in advance, ever. I engage the material and see where it leads me. If you want to know why so many of my books are so weird, why, now you know!

comments.title: Egregores/Magical Groups
comments.content:
Greetings John!
This is a bit of a convoluted question, but I’ll try to keep it simple. I suppose this has to do with magic in a much broader sense. I wanted to see what you thought of this. I’m still quite green, but in having interest in Neoplatonism and Kabbalah I’ve of course invested some time into the golden dawn. But I’m realizing that the source material that the order bases its teachings on, at least some of them, are either incorrect or really strange interpretations. Take the correspondence given to the seven double letters. I do realize the safer get Sepher Yetzirah manuscripts can disagree with one another.. What I’m trying to get at here is do mistakes like this affect the efficiency of the magic within the order? Or if the egregore is strong enough, does the fact that this information is believed and practiced consistently over time overcome that shortcoming? I’ll tack this last question on as well, was the golden Dan influenced more by the new thought movement in terms of its interpretation of older systems like Neoplatonism and Hermeticism? Does the order represent these ideas properly, is what I’m getting at. Thank you for your time John. Hope you’re doing good!

replies.title: Re: Egregores/Magical Groups
replies.content:
Symbolism is a tool, not a truth, and so there are no wrong symbols. There are, for example, at least a dozen ways to apply the Hebrew alphabet to the Tree of Life, and they all work -- what's more, they work equally well. Quarrels among the partisans of different systems are like a quarrel between a German, a Frenchman, and an Englishman about whether the four-legged animal barking at them is ein Hund, un chien, or a dog.

The GD drew its symbolism from one particular corner of mostly British occultism with close ties to some of the odder ends of Christian Cabala. Other traditions draw from other places and have their own oddities. None of it matters in practice; any symbolic system studied and practiced assiduously will have comparable effects, just as you can say "that's a dog" in any human language.

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content:
It's weird that you bring that up in a reply to a comment mentioning "mooching", since that's something she tried to exclude from her system -- she dragged her idea of the proper conception of "charity" over to being something people do to (really "with", since it's a voluntary transaction) other people exhibiting the same life-oriented (meaning, in her system, worldly-productive-mechanical-consequence-oriented) values, as a device for maintaining those values in oneself against (implied) entropy and dispiriting examples from other people. (I suppose that could be undiplomatically characterized as like exchanging money for ongoing performance-art equivalents of (tasteful and refined) inspirational workplace and home-office posters not otherwise available, at a cheaper price.)

While this isn't saying much, since she found something about a lot of things that she found revolting (she had some kind of BPD-like splitting), I half-expect that she would have found something in Satanism to find revolting, even the ostensibly non-mooching form.

And yet... I guess there was that one journal entry where she judged the attitude of Leopold and Loeb towards the murder they committed to contain, to put it a charitably, a distilled essence of something vitally important that she was uniformly not permitted to get under the Soviet cultural machine -- something to do with it being notionally fundamentally legitimate to have a psychological base from which one could detect and reject toxic reality-untethered consensus. Having seen that attitude up close in connection with Satanism(-broadly-construed), I can see how the one could be interpreted by a Satanist as being fulfilled in the other, especially if you lean into interpreting its essence as defiance/opposition instead of unconcern/independence. A lot of Rand's philosophy could be framed as an attempt to worldbuild an entire hypothetical culture under which that impulse could plausibly be consistently "okay" or "laudable".

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content: You might also factor in her glorification of William Hickman, one of the most revolting child murderers of the century. (Or for that matter the fact that she went on Social Security and Medicare as soon as she was eligible for them -- apparently her dislike of mooching only applied to other people.)

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content:
Oops, sorry, I guess it was that case. I'm not sure how I got the wires crossed.

To all appearances, she considered accepting welfare to be a substitute way of accepting restitution.

> The recipient [...] is morally justified only so long as he regards it as restitution and opposes all forms of welfare statism. [...] If this sounds like a paradox, the fault lies in the moral contradictions of welfare statism[...]
>
> [...a bunch of invocation of elsewhere-vehemently-argued-to-more-properly-be-quite-exacting conceptions of "rights"...] The victims do not have to add self-inflicted martyrdom to the injury done to them by others; they do not have to let the looters profit doubly, by letting them distribute the money exclusively to the parasites who clamored for it. Whenever the welfare-state laws offer them some small restitution, the victims should take it[...]

I'm not sure what she would have said about a proposal for everyone to do an accounting to divide their taxes into just and unjust parts and only accept "restitution" up to the quantity calculated as unjust so far, or up to the net-present value of some future expected amount of unjust expropriation. But she did have her goodguys in Atlas Shrugged do that calculation for the heroine and pay her out of, I think it might have been wealth taken by piracy from shipping of goods bought by states they considered anti-life outlaws, suggesting that the idea was significantly older than her turning 65: roughly speaking, that accepting welfare were restorative piracy by other means.

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content: Oh, I know. To my mind it's simply a flurry of excuses for benefiting from an institution she claimed to oppose.

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content:
In the scenario where trying to "benefit" on net from the institution, relative to how much she suffered from it, was her actual motivation, and everything else was excuses, then you should also expect that, ex ante, she would have come up excuses to support the creation of the institution if it hadn't existed already and would only benefit her if it did.

Or in the more plausible ex post scenario, you should also expect that, if for some reason someone had done the calculation of unjust-taxedness for her (and others who she might redistribute any excess "restitution" to), and if for some reason she got more than that amount in welfare payments, she would in that case reliably generate further excuses within that flurry for keeping the extra money as well, without even distributing it to ideologically allied followers who hadn't gotten enough welfare moneys back yet.

Of course we can't know now what she would have done in that hypothetical, but you should notice whether or not that further prediction within the hypothetical would feel discordant with what else you know of her. To my mind, if I'm trying to get it to feel even only just moderately discordant, I have to add the proviso where I suppose she's assigning the extra moneys to an at least half-formed subjective equivalent of a separately-tracked earmarked account for activism (against statism or for philosophical advocacy that seemed better-leveraged against statism), or to an activist organization she controlled. If I don't add that proviso it feels pretty discordant.

Maybe to your mind it doesn't feel discordant, because you expect that she's coming from a place of ordinary unbalanced selfishness.

But, side evidence about what Rand would have rationalized aside, it would be weird for there to be no such viable moral category, in anybody's moral psychology, as "'benefiting' from an institution one claims to oppose, as constructed by an observer by defining 'benefit' relative to a privileged 'null-action' baseline alternative of only just suffering from that institution on net, and carefully only benefiting up to a point of equal restitution", recognizable as distinct from "willingly benefiting from an institution even when it's on net, but claiming to oppose it". So it seems like there at least ought to be some case where, if someone observes conduct toward the welfare system like Rand's, and it's observational evidence which doesn't distinguish between the two positions, it should be possible for there to exist side evidence about the person's character to establish that actually the moral category they were using was the first one and not the second one.

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content: To my mind, the categorical imperative applies here. If you're going to insist that nobody should benefit from a given thing, that implies that you yourself should not benefit from it. If I insisted that television was a bad thing and nobody should watch it, but watched television regularly just because it exists, I'd be a hypocrite. As I see it, the same logic applies to Rand.

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content: I find it baffling how many people will take advantage of things they claim to abhor. Whether it's wealthy people claiming tax credits and complaining their tax bill is lower than their employees, people who claim to hate governments subsidizing things taking advantage of government subsidies, people who claim to be concerned about the environment flying for vacation, or people who claim to hate AI using it, it's astonishing how many people seem to think "If it's there, and it benefits me, I must use it, regardless of whether I think it moral or not." It's always struck me as bizarre, to put it mildly.

replies.title: Re: Anton Shrugged
replies.content:
Reading further, I guess the essay containing that text itself also wasn't that long after Atlas Shrugged; it was only the compilation whose date of publication was after her death.

(trying to summarize https://medium.com/the-radical-center/ayn-rand-nietzsche-and-the-purposeless-monster-e0e8e314398a )

There may have been a game of telephone from Rand's journals going on about Hickman, to Jennifer Burns's 2009 biography "Goddess of the Market", to a prominent 2010 Alternet article.

Parts of her journals published after her death in 1996 include parts that Burns didn't mention, where she called Hickman a "purposeless monster" and explicitly disconnected Hickman's motives and specific style of murder from those of a closer-to-properly-idealized serial killer (?) protagonist in an early novel plot she gave up on, which plot (reading between the lines) she had been trying to construct as a condemnation of a (hypothetical clearer-cut?) society that had convergently prevented the protagonist from having any other (recognizable?) psychologically viable way of internally siding with what she considered to be the only fundamentally healthy configuration of internal psychological space-making.

> all the information purported to show Rand’s evil views comes from her own journals. Yet, what they [the purporters] don’t quote is Rand’s own appraisal of the relationship of Renahan to Hickman. She said Renahan is “very far from him [Hickman], of course. The outside of Hickman, but not the inside. Much deeper and much more. A Hickman with a purpose. And without the degeneracy. It is more exact to say that the model is not Hickman, but what Hickman suggested to me.” [emphasis probably Peron's]

Which doesn't directly say she understood that actually Hickman was a psychopath in whatever terms, only a 'degenerate', but does strongly suggest that she understood that Hickman himself was actually internally constituted some other way than the thing she was supposing it would instead be good to affirm.

The article also raises various evidences about how how her views changed over time in other respects.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is the purpose of prayer?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That depends very much on what you mean by that much-muddled word. If you mean the sort of "Lord gimme this, Lord gimme that" crap enshrined in hymns such as "Sweet Hour of Prayer" --

"That bids me at my Father's throne
Make all my wants and wishes known"

-- it's an exercise in overinflated entitlement and inevitable frustration. The Divine is not a vending machine.

True prayer is the act of spending time in the company of some aspect of the Divine. It's at least as much a matter of listening as of talking. Approach the deity to whom you pray as you would a much older and wiser friend and you're likely to get it right. Speak and then listen attentively, or just be in the presence of the deity.

Maybe you had the experience in childhood of sitting with a beloved grandparent. The two of you might not have exchanged more than a few words, if any; there you were, side by side on the sofa; Grandpa was reading the newspaper, and you were reading a picture book and slowly piecing together what the letters spelt out -- and yet the memory is one of great closeness and comfort. That's a very powerful kind of prayer. Mystics call it the prayer of simple regard, or when it's turned into a sustained discipline, the practice of the presence of God.

The important point is that you build a relationship with the Divine the way you build any other relationship, by spending time with the other person. Prayer in all its many forms is how you do that with the Divine.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) One of the great differences between standard Abrahamic monotheism and traditional polytheism is that polytheism doesn't postulate the vast and irreconcilable difference between deity and humanity that most monotheist faiths do. The gods and goddesses are greater, wiser, and mightier than we are, but the difference isn't absolute; there are greater and lesser gods, and some of the lesser ones are much closer to the human level. As for what they might want from us, I can think of no better explanation than one I put into one of my novels:

*****
She laughed, shaking her head in amazement, then considered the vast span of time she’d been shown, the infinitesimal length of her own life, and the greater but still tiny span allotted to her species, and asked in wonder: why do you even bother with us?

The answer this time came in the form of one of her own memories. That autumn, looking out the window of her bedroom in the house on Halsey Street, she’d seen a bright-eyed little wren hopping here and there on the outside sill, and watched it for most of fifteen minutes until it finally darted off in a flurry of tiny wings. The image came back to her in perfect clarity, vanished as suddenly as it had come.

The image delighted her, and she laughed again. A sudden stray thought tried to convince her that she should take offense at the comparison, brought up all those claims she’d heard that human beings had some grander role in the scheme of things than wrens or hermit crabs or faint shapeless blurs at the bottom of Archaean tidepools; a slogan from a poster on a Miskatonic University bulletin board tried to force its way into her mind. At that moment, it might as well have tried to convince her that squares have five sides and rocks fall straight up into the sky. I hope we entertain you, she said, still laughing.

Now and again, the not-voice responded, amused. Then, serious again: But there is another matter. Some things can be done by a wren that a human cannot do as easily, or at all.

She had wondered, since her first contact with Saint Toad, if one of the doors that would open before her would lead her to do things at the Great Old One’s direction. She could refuse, she knew that at once, and there might be good reason to do so, but the thought was a distant abstraction. If there’s anything you want me to do...

All in good time, the Great Old One replied.

*****
2) Prayer is the standard approach. You might consider setting up a little shrine -- a sacred image and an incense burner are quite enough to start with -- and taking a little while every day to pray there.

3) Well, I recall scraps of some of my previous lives, so I expect to go on to some other incarnation. Still, we'll see!

“Beware St. Toad’s cracked chimes!” I heard him scream
As I plunged into those mad lanes that wind
In labyrinths obscure and undefined
South of the river where old centuries dream.
He was a furtive figure, bent and ragged,

I paused, when a third greybeard croaked in fear:

Till suddenly that black spire loomed ahead.

comments.title: Religion, alienation, and the turning of an age.
comments.content:
Hello Mr Greer.

I was listening to your recent talk with plant cunning while doing some throughly boring manual labor and was struck by a comment you made about axial religion. You mentioned that axial religion is very focused on uniformity of everything that believers engage in. This seemed to express about 90% of everything I’ve struggled with in a long and maybe still ongoing journey in Christianity. Namely that I can’t be bothered to condemn people that seem to be faithful to my God in a different manner and am continually confused as to why pastors and laymen alike seemed dead set on convincing me to be faithful towards said God in ways that damage my own faith.
This has led me to two hypotheses about the way I think religion is going to go in the next few centuries. Firstly religion is going to get weird I suspect the Protestant inclination in the US to spilt into an infinite spectrum of tiny denominations that all are generally cordial will continue. This will be joined by a host of new traditions that may or may not give any credibility to axial era religion or tradition.
Secondly that the idea of a war of religion will dissipate, in the west at least, due to the enspectrumification of religion. I see this as a great thing religious war had tended to be a great excuse for committing horrific war crimes and the less of those the better. Now if you’re the kind of young man that is reconstructing his entire identity around a mental image of the halcyon religious past that is rapidly filling up the conservative end of religion today this is probably less good news.
Finally I think we have already seen this beginning to take effect. Most notably that stalwart of the full flowering of axial religion the Catholic Church is allowing a remarkable amount of liturgical variation since Vatican 2 and seems to be open to reabsorbing its rightward schismatics if they would calm down a little.
I have no idea of I’ve hit the nail on the head or am wandering into a swamp but this line of thinking has helped my own religious life and I hope will help others suffering the cognitive dissonance of having a non-axial mind while trying to live in an axial religion. I also would like to say that I don’t think the next great age of this chunk of mankind will be a utopian garden we are still monkeys with rocks (very fancy rocks but still rocks) and humans will sadly always find reasons to hate rob and kill each other.

JL

replies.title: Re: Religion, alienation, and the turning of an age.
replies.content:
This is certainly what I suspect. We're moving out of the Piscean age, which belongs to the element of water and is ruled by Neptune; water corresponds to emotions and Neptune is the planet of unities, so powerful emotional pressures toward unity and uniformity have typified Piscean religion. The coming Aquarian age belongs to the element of air and is ruled by Uranus, the planet of individuality and eccentricity; I expect the religions of the next 2160 years to be more intellectual than emotional and much more diverse, focusing on each individual following his or her own spiritual vision.

I think a significant minority of people are already becoming attuned to the new influence, and find themselves alienated from the Axial/Piscean faiths. The current movement toward religious conservatism is of course also a factor; the Second Religiosity, as Oswald Spengler called it, is an attempt to stave off social and mental chaos by returning to a stable source of meaning and value. It's an important and generally beneficial part of the declining phase of every great civilization, but as it proceeds it'll become more and more a habit of the overrprivileged classes, while strange new religious visions take shape down in the crawlspaces of society among the disenfranchised and underprivileged masses. That is to say, things will get strange as we proceed.

replies.title: Re: Religion, alienation, and the turning of an age.
replies.content:
One of the things that stands out to me is that during the vast majority of the Piscean age, Neptune was unknown and so its influence unaccounted for, which is likely why the religions of that age all seemed to have developed the awkward habit of seeing themselves as paradigms of what all religions are like — or even as just religion, singular — instead of the very exceptional oddballs they actually are in the grand scheme of history.

This time even the return to religious conservatism will be with a self-consciousness of their own distinctness and exceptional character, even if what they take from it is that they are uniquely true and wonderful. Christians (etc.) of the future may well still judge other religions by their own lights, but the world where they insist on interpreting other religions by their own lights is arguably fading out for good.

Prayer works for this, in its repetitive variety though not exactly because of it's intended purpose. Repetitive action puts the rational mind to sleep as the brain focuses on keeping a rhythm. That's why its easy to fall asleep while doing it and several tricks have been to be devised to keep people awake! This also helps because if you go to sleep with the rational mind still active, you don't rest, it remains active even if you are asleep (this is why people with anxiety report sleeping but not resting). Another way of doing it is controlled breathing. Try this, sit in a comfortable position, back straight unsupported and cross your feet at the ankles, right over left. Fold the index and middle finger if your right hand and keep the others extended. You will use the little and anular finger to block the left nostril and the thumb to close the right nostril. Keep the left hand on your lap facing upwards. For the process itself, close your eyes and block the right nostril with your thumb, inhale deeply hold for a moment and exhale through the same nostril. Then you switch and block the left nostril with the little and anular finger and open the right nostril to do the same. Inhale and exhale from the same nostril, and switch. You can do this as much as you like. You only need to remember two things, you always switch after an exhalation and you breath as slowly as it is possible for you without having to gasp or break the stream of breathing. If you are able to focus on the breath without allowing the mind to keep agitating things you will start to get relaxed in 10-15 minutes.

This last is called the cleansing breath and it works by using excess energy to clean your energy body so it's both healthy and relaxing. There are two big pipes in the energy body linked to the nostrils, by doing it slowly it would be like cleaning and releasing the pressure of a pipe by opening the valves and having water go up and down. This is also safe with ceremonial magic because it doesn't force the energy towards the other energy pathways and it also focuses you back into the body.

comments.title: Relationship Divination
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I'd like to once again ask for your help regarding divination with the SGO. Some weeks ago, to recap, I prayed to Freyja for some advice about my love life (or lack thereof) and, to my surprise, dreamt the entire night about a friend. As I hadn't thought about her that way before, I consulted the oracle and decided that this was more complex than "you should romantically pursue her" because the card I pulled for a relationship between us was Limitation.

Unfortunately, there was no way to put the genie back into its bottle and now I am thinking about her that way. So, naturally, I started wondering whether Limitation was a firm no or even a no at all or whether I just misinterpreted it, ranging from "it wouldn't be everything you're hoping for" to contortions like "our relationship right now is limited to being platonic."

I wanted more clarity and so I pulled another spread yesterday:

Me: The Pentagram: Power
Her: The Double Square reversed: Risk
A relationship between us: The Unmarked Card reversed: Hiddenness
What does Hiddenness mean?: The Sphere reversed: Unknowability

(By the way, "Unknowability" isn't printed upside down on the card, which seems like one of those mistakes you wanted to fix in the new edition you were thinking about.)

1) Was this an advisable way to go about this situation? I know I'm skirting dangerously close to "I don't like the cards, so I'll just pull another spread."

2) Are clarification questions like the fourth card a good strategy? I'm pretty sure I've always pulled a card that feels pretty close to the one I'm asking about, which makes sense but also doesn't seem too helpful.

I'd read this with a slightly positive spin because the Power to bring about a desired outcome lies with me. I'm both less happy and less sure about Risk; perhaps she would be taking a risk? Does that make sense? Would you read this differently?

comments.title: Personal Pseudomorphosis
comments.content:
It was quite interesting to hear your discussion with Nick Land last night, looking forward to more, it seemed like it was more him interviewing you, and you both seemed to agree on a lot more than I would have thought. I think personally, by the way he was asking questions, that he may agree more with you than he may even let on. But, anyway,

Speaking of Spengler, I have been thinking about my personal exploration and practice of spiritual systems as something like pseudomorphoses. With the self-inquiry tradition of Richard Rose, which I see as sort of my base system, then Heathen esotericism, Buddhist meditation, Astrological magic, Heathen Golden Dawn, and now this Vedic lineage (which I'm in my 4th year of study). I have gone deeply into each of these, for several years of intensive practice each. I notice that each system has profoundly changed the way I perceive and relate to reality. I find, especially in the Vedic lineage, as it is more foreign to me, I am learning more of a "way of thinking" - "tools not truths". I also realize this kind of process is not for everybody, and is also perhaps only possible in the kind of age we are currenttly living in (similar to the last change of astrological age) As an American, born into an infant culture, I kind of feel like I'm doing a pseudomorphosis in the microcosm, does this make sense to you?

replies.title: Re: Personal Pseudomorphosis
replies.content:
1) We have different visions of history but both are founded on similar epistemologies -- that is, we both realize that human beings have no access to objective reality, we both understand that the world inhabited by human beings is constructed from mythic narratives, and we both have some sense of the ways that the narrative universe can be shaped by what we may as well call magic. That's a lot of common ground! Land is more or less committed to an apocalyptic narrative, and I'm at least as firmly committed to an antiapocalyptic narrative, but we can easily bracket those differences and talk about subjects where our takes cast light on each other.

2) That's one of the essential roles of intellectuals in any culture. Their personal pseudomorphoses may come from the past -- think of English scholars in the 18th century learning Greek and Latin -- or from other cultures -- think of their descendants a century later grappling with Sanskrit literature -- but that process of absorbing outside perspectives is an important part of how every culture ripens its intellectual heritage.

replies.title: Re: Personal Pseudomorphosis
replies.content: Richard Rose founded the TAT Foundation, is that right? Does that tradition go in the direction of non-dualism?

replies.title: Re: Personal Pseudomorphosis
replies.content: Yes, I've been involved with TAT since I was in college. It definitely goes in the direction of non-dualism, but it's very different than the modern neo-advaita stuff. His teaching was very influenced by Gurdjieff, Zen, Ramana Maharshi, Esoteric Christianity, Magic, and Gnosticism (he called his first book "The Albigen Papers" after the Cathars.)

replies.title: Re: Personal Pseudomorphosis
replies.content: The thought of a nondualist Catharism makes my head hurt...

replies.title: Re: Personal Pseudomorphosis
replies.content:
To be fair, I don't know how much of a Cathar he actually was, though he probably was one in a past life.

He had a pretty grim view of the material world, and the Powers in control, in many ways, but the nondual teachings came out of a direct experience he had at age 30 that took him some years to recover from. He was a very interesting person; a hard-working West Virginian who built his own house, raised goats, and shot back at the local hillbillies who didn't like the hippies that would visit him on his farm (there was a court battle about it that he won, self-defense.) He also had some psychic powers and would perform exorcisms and healings. I'd consider him a special kind of American saint.

I've recently realized something unsettling: there's a very good case to be made that the people who created the Magician States knew what they were doing. I recently did a dive into the history of the telecommunications industry, and found that much of it ties back to Lord Kitchener, David Sarnoff, and George Creel. Essentially, these three people created the modern propaganda state, and everyone else copied them. Kitchener proved its power by using posters and cinema to convince the public to violently oppose the pacifist movement and to boost military recruiting; Creel demonstrated its power to shape public opinion by convincing millions of Americans to abandon their prior views and embrace a military invention when, mere months earlier public opinion was opposed to it, all while the president had campaigned on keeping the country out of the war; and after the war, Sarnoff leveraged the new technology of radio to create a massive business empire based on broadcasting advertising to the masses.

All three of them have ties to occultism: Sarnoff spent years being raised by a Rabbi who lived in Minsk when that was a hotbed of Jewish Kabbalah (and spent much of his life doing everything he could to hide the identity of said Rabbi and what he learned there); Lord Kitchner learned Arabic while living in Egypt and became a collector of old books, and upon his death it appears that a number of Arabic occult books were in his library, including the Picatrix; and George Creel published Rosicrucian works (such as Quatrains of Christ) prior to his involvement in the Committee on Public Relations.

I've read Eros and Magic in the Renaissance, and will read the Hounds of Actaeon in the next few weeks, but those books are on the links between the system and occultism; not between any of the individuals who played a key role in creating it and occultism. Do you know if anyone else has explored possible links between key figures in the early history of mass media and occultism?
